questions.question: The word 'soccer' is often thought of as American — but it actually originated in 19th-century England as slang. From which official name of the sport was it derived?
questions.reasoning: Debunks a widespread myth ('soccer is American') with a clean reveal. Universally relatable and easy to share.

questions.question: Liverpool FC's iconic anthem 'You'll Never Walk Alone' wasn't written for football. It comes from which 1945 Rodgers and Hammerstein musical?
questions.reasoning: Bridges football and musical theatre — universally surprising. Answer 'Carousel' is recoverable for many; the journey is the joy.

questions.question: Yellow and red cards were introduced to football at the 1970 World Cup. According to referee Ken Aston, who created them, what everyday object inspired the colour scheme?
questions.reasoning: Standout. Mundane origin for one of the game's most iconic visual symbols. High wow factor, easy answer, perfect for telling at the table.

questions.question: True or false: The English Premier League is younger than the original Star Wars trilogy.
questions.reasoning: Cross-domain comparison frame. True/false is driving-perfect; the fact subverts how 'eternal' the PL feels.

questions.question: The dramatic Champions League anthem you hear before kickoff was composed by Tony Britten in 1992. He based it on which famous Baroque coronation anthem written by George Frideric Handel?
questions.reasoning: Royal coronation music → biggest stage in club football. Even if you don't know Zadok the Priest, the connection is electric. Slightly long for driving.

questions.question: When the European Cup (now the Champions League) launched in 1955-56, one club won the first five editions in a row — a streak no other club has ever matched. Which club?
questions.reasoning: Recall-leaning — most football fans guess Real Madrid immediately. The 'never matched' frame helps but doesn't elevate it past average.

questions.question: Brazil's iconic Maracanã stadium was completed in time for the 1950 World Cup final between Brazil and Uruguay. The official attendance is recorded at 173,850 — but unofficially, some estimates say how many people were actually in the ground?
questions.reasoning: Wow-factor crowd record, but the question carries two big numbers (173,850 + answer) which is heavy mental load while driving. Revise to drop the official figure from the question.

questions.question: When Germany famously thrashed Brazil 7-1 in the 2014 World Cup semi-final, something extraordinary happened in the first half. How quickly did Germany score their middle five goals?
questions.reasoning: The pace of the collapse is stunning, but 'middle five goals' phrasing is fiddly to parse aurally. Revise to clarify the time window.

questions.question: Pelé is one of the most recognised names in football — but he hated the nickname as a child and never knew what it meant. What is his actual birth name?
questions.reasoning: The 'even he didn't know what it meant' hook elevates a recall fact to a story. Long Brazilian name is OK aurally.

questions.question: Manchester United was founded in 1878 — but not as a football club for the city. It started as a railway-workers' team named after the local depot. What was its original name?
questions.reasoning: Working-class railway origin for a global mega-club. Hard but accessible — anyone can guess from context, the reveal lands.

questions.question: FIFA finally introduced goal-line technology to the World Cup in 2014. The push to do so was hugely accelerated by which English midfielder's clearly-over-the-line goal that was disallowed against Germany at the 2010 World Cup?
questions.reasoning: Strong cause-and-effect narrative but the question is long and skews English. Revise to tighten phrasing — international audience may not recall Lampard as readily.

questions.question: The first FIFA World Cup was held in 1930 in Uruguay. Two coincidences made the tiny South American nation an obvious choice — they were the reigning Olympic football champions, and they were celebrating which milestone that year?
questions.reasoning: Solid origin story but the setup is dense ('two coincidences', 'reigning Olympic champions', 'milestone') — too much to hold in audio memory. Trim to a single hook.

questions.question: Glasgow's 'Old Firm' derby between Celtic and Rangers is one of football's most intense rivalries. Beyond geography, the two clubs were historically aligned with which divide?
questions.reasoning: Touches on a sensitive cultural divide; substantial educational value but the answer is well-known among football fans, blunting surprise. Revise to add a specific founding-fact angle.

questions.question: AC Milan, despite being a quintessentially Italian club, was founded in 1899 by two Englishmen — and uses the English spelling of the city's name to this day. What is the city actually called in Italian?
questions.reasoning: Surprising English founding paired with naming quirk — strong content. Setup is long; revise to tighten ('founded by Englishmen — what's the Italian spelling of the city?').

questions.question: The football term 'hat-trick' for three goals didn't start in football. It originated in which other sport in the 1850s, where a player taking three wickets in three balls was traditionally rewarded with a hat?
questions.reasoning: Etymology bridges two sports with a literal hat. The wicket clue half-gives it away but that's the design — universal appeal.

questions.question: At Euro 2004, the bookmakers' 150-1 outsiders pulled off one of football's greatest tournament shocks — beating the host nation in both the opening match and the final. Which country won?
questions.reasoning: Underdog narrative is strong; '150-1 outsiders' is a great hook. Just shy of approve — revise by leaning into the symmetry of beating Portugal twice.

questions.question: At the 2022 FIFA World Cup in Qatar, an African nation reached the semi-finals for the first time in the tournament's 92-year history. Which country?
questions.reasoning: Recent and recall-heavy; followers of the World Cup will answer instantly. Revise with a more surprising angle (e.g., who they beat on the way).

questions.question: Brazil entered the 1950 World Cup final at the Maracanã needing only a draw to win the tournament. Uruguay won 2-1 — a national tragedy that became known by what one-word Portuguese name?
questions.reasoning: Cultural-fallout angle (kit colours changed afterward) is rich. Word 'Maracanazo' is exotic but memorable. Long setup but compelling.

questions.question: Italians don't call the sport 'football' — they call it 'calcio', which literally means 'kick'. The name comes from a much older ball game played by which Renaissance city's nobility, who used it to celebrate Carnival in costumed teams?
questions.reasoning: Renaissance pageantry → modern Italian football. Florence is universally famous. Question is long but the payoff justifies.

questions.question: A 17-year-old Pelé scored a hat-trick in the semi-final and two more in the final to lead Brazil to its first World Cup title in 1958. Which European host nation did he beat in the final?
questions.reasoning: Pure recall once you know Pelé won 1958 in Europe. The age-17 frame helps but the answer slot is small. Revise toward the specific looped-volley goal.

questions.question: At the 1954 World Cup final, the heavily favoured Hungarian 'Mighty Magyars' — unbeaten in 32 matches — were defeated 3-2 in a stunning upset that became known as the 'Miracle of Bern'. Which country pulled off the shock win?
questions.reasoning: Classic upset narrative; 'Miracle of Bern' phrase is lovely. Revise to remove some scaffolding (32-match streak + score + nickname is dense).

questions.question: The first football match ever broadcast on television, in September 1937, wasn't a major fixture — it was a friendly between the first team and reserves of which English club, broadcast experimentally by the BBC?
questions.reasoning: Birth of TV football from a glorified training game is delightful. Scaffolding 'first team and reserves' is overlong; revise to shorter setup.

questions.question: The phrase 'the beautiful game' as a synonym for football was popularised globally by which Brazilian legend's 1977 autobiography?
questions.reasoning: Recall: 'Brazilian legend's 1977 autobiography' all but names Pelé. Revise to anchor on the title 'My Life and the Beautiful Game' with less hand-holding.

questions.question: True or false: Penalty shootouts were not used to decide knockout matches in the FIFA World Cup until 1982 — before that, drawn matches were settled by a coin toss or replay.
questions.reasoning: Hard to believe pre-1982 drawn matches could be settled by lots. True/false drives perfectly; the coin-toss detail is the kicker.
